Application/Control Number: 16/123,456                            Page 4
Art Unit: 2194

                             OFFICE ACTION SUMMARY

Examiner's Amendments/Comments:
________________________________

Claims 1-20 are pending; of the pending claims 1, 6, and 11 are
independent.

                          DETAILED ACTION

Rejection Under 35 U.S.C. § 112(b) — Indefiniteness

Claims 1, 6, and 11 are rejected under 35 U.S.C. § 112(b) as being
indefinite for failing to particularly point out and distinctly claim
the subject matter which the inventor regards as the invention.

Regarding claim 1, the phrase "processing the data in a manner optimized
for the given context" is unclear because one of ordinary skill in the
art would not understand the metes and bounds of the term "optimized for
the given context." The claim language does not provide antecedent basis
for the limitation "said given context" which first appears in claim 1,
line 7 without prior introduction. The examiner finds that this renders
the claim indefinite.

Applicant is required to particularly point out and distinctly claim the
subject matter of the invention.


Rejection Under 35 U.S.C. § 103 — Obviousness

Claims 1-5 are rejected under 35 U.S.C. § 103 as being unpatentable
over Smith et al. (US 9,876,543) in view of Jones (US 8,765,432).

Smith discloses a digital data processing system comprising a processor
configured to execute instructions (col. 3, ll. 12-25), a memory storing
a plurality of executable modules (col. 4, ll. 1-18), and a network
interface adapted to transmit data packets to a remote server (col. 5,
ll. 3-9). Smith further teaches dynamically allocating resources based on
current workload conditions (col. 6, ll. 20-35).

It would have been obvious to one of ordinary skill in the art at the
time of the invention to modify Smith's resource allocation mechanism with
the load-balancing algorithm taught by Jones (col. 2, ll. 10-25) because
Jones teaches that such modifications yield predictably improved
throughput performance. One of ordinary skill in the art would have
been motivated to combine the teachings because both references are
directed to the same technical field of distributed computing systems
and address the same recognized problem of resource utilization.


Rejection Under 35 U.S.C. § 102 — Anticipation

Claim 6 is rejected under 35 U.S.C. § 102(a)(1) as being anticipated by
the disclosure of Williams (US 10,234,567).

Regarding the limitation "a hardware accelerator configured to perform
matrix multiplication operations in parallel using a systolic array
architecture," Williams explicitly discloses (at col. 8, ll. 4-19):
"The processing unit includes dedicated silicon logic for parallel matrix
multiplication implemented via a systolic array." This disclosure directly
anticipates the claimed limitation. Williams teaches each and every
element of claim 6 as discussed in detail below.

Regarding the limitation "wherein the systolic array comprises at least
256 processing elements arranged in a two-dimensional grid," Williams
discloses a 16×16 array of processing elements (col. 8, ll. 20-30),
which constitutes a 256-element two-dimensional systolic array, thereby
teaching each element of the claimed limitation.


Any inquiry concerning this communication or earlier communications from
the examiner should be directed to the primary examiner whose name and
telephone number appear below.

Primary Examiner — Technology Center 2100
